speaker: interviewer
text: The date is April 1st, 1996. We left off, on the last tape, talking about barracks with Jehovah Witness-- Witnesses in them. Did you personally know any Jehovah Witnesses? Or did you know anyone in these barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That's what the barbed wire was for. You could not talk with them. They couldn't talk to us. Being seen near them was even punishable. They were completely isolated because they, being [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] religiously motivated to help others, were completely separated so that they could not contaminate the other prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know they were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- we marched by every day. And we saw them marching by. They would do hard labor. So they had to get in and out of their barracks. And some of them who worked outside-- out of the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in any hard labor while you were in Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what kind of work were you doing, yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did nothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just I'd be worked on, so to speak. It was strictly passive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did something.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you have to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: But that was-- being one of the youngest and the strongest, I always went for the food. I had to carry very heavy pockets, and they insisted that we did not rest until we walked or ran. We could not walk. We had to run across the parade crowds until we came to the back street. We could not set them down. If we set the bucket down, they said it's too heavy for you. It's a [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in charge of getting the food.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not in charge, but one of the detail.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the food? Where did you have to go to get it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was at the administration building in the kitchen, which was on the other side of the parade route.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with anybody at all that was very well known, or a famous personality, infamous personality? Yes. It depends. Famous for who? I would like to tell you a little story.

speaker: interviewee
text: During Christmas time, I was [INAUDIBLE]. It was a day of rest. And a few inmates-- prisoners, Jews-- put up a little sketch and [INAUDIBLE] others in various [? parks ?] to entertain us. One of us was a famous bass from the upper of Prague. His name was [? Joseph ?] Goodman. There was another Goodman in Berlin, who was a famous baritone, William Goodman-- Wilhelm Goodman. And he sang for us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can-- he sang for us [SPEAKING FRENCH], which is a patriotic French song, and which has as its bass melody. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And as he came to the end, which [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], he lowered his voice, so it couldn't be heard and recognized. He died later, there. Another one was a famous entertainer from Vienna, who [? at one ?] [? time ?] entertained us. His name was Leopold [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: Whobaum?

speaker: interviewee
text: [GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was very humorous and really made us almost laugh. He also died later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right after?

speaker: interviewee
text: The story about those two was-- I know definitely of [? Joseph ?] Goodman-- he was in the same barrack as I was-- that he had non-Jewish wife, who hated him or wanted to divorce him. She wanted to get rid of him, whatever it is. And she wouldn't do anything to help him. And maybe even she made sure he wouldn't get out. He was-- he never had-- he had no family, nobody to do anything for him. And he was extremely lonely and depressed. And I have a suspicion that the situation with [GERMAN] was similar.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of entertainment did-- you said they put on a little sketch. Can you give me an-- a little bit more detail?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a monologue. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Goodman, he sang a song without accompaniment. He sang a few others, too, all without accompaniment. [GERMAN] recited two or three of his own monologues, for which he was famous.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any monologue that you could recite?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know the words. I know the content of it. It was a text, but that's-- that's actually material. It cannot be duplicated.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened-- was there any kind of medical treatment in Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They had a very fine medical clinic. There was only one thing wrong. If you reported for sick-- sick hall, they called you a faker and punished you. So it was very dangerous to go to-- to go to sick hall.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I tell one story. I don't want to [INAUDIBLE]. It's [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my brother, where we lived in the United States, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] went to a party in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] New York. He was a bachelor, and he met another-- there was a group of fellows. And if-- somebody called out. He said, your name is Selling. He says, yes. You know Martin Selling?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was a little dramatic. He said, he saved my life. What actually happened for us, he-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [DRINKS] he got sick with a cold. And he was afraid, rightly so, to go to sick hall. And I-- having a little bit of experience with my mother being sick, I took care of him. And we actually made it possible for him to stay around and not to sick hall for two or three days until he got better. And I took care of him at that time, made sure he got something to eat and whatever is necessary. What's parallel is another fellow got extremely sick. He also-- also a young kid. He does not-- he didn't want to go to sick hall. I took care of him. I'll just help you a little, too. But everybody was afraid of infections. Somehow, I was not. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] finally, we had to give him up. He was diagnosed with meningitis. And this camp doctor, who was proud of his profession, did everything to pull him through, and he pulled him through, while hundreds around him died from neglect.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the camp doctor, was he a prisoner?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was an SS man.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was in the SS.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was an SS man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --go in for any kind of medical treatment?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I did not.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you help to take care of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I made sure he had blankets, made sure he had water. And when he was restless, I washed his face and-- all-- what you do with a person with a high temperature.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of any kind of resistance movement of any type whatsoever inside Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was none. There couldn't have been one.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till the 27th of January, 1939, which was five weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And under what circumstances did you leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was released, as I told you-- released periodically in drips and drops. And while I was in Dachau, my aunt got-- and other relatives, got permission from a refugee camp in England that I can go there till my registration number for the United States came up. And I was told I have to wait several years before I finally could get in under the quota system. You're familiar with the quota system, I presume? Yeah. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: You can talk about the quota system.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't want to talk about it. It's generally known. And there are better sources in that respect. I have to keep it personal. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was released under the condition that I leave Germany within six months. And the only place I could go was a refugee camp. And that was a condition that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Under which I was released.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your friend Ernst-- was he released with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was released three days earlier.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was he released to?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had a chance to come to the United States. And we met up again in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you left other people behind.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were released--

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, I was the last of those eight.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were the last of the eight to be released.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go as soon as you were released?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Munich and Lehrberg. In fact, we had to leave Lehrberg because the house was confiscated and everything. And I went to my aunt, where my brother lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the whole time you were in Dachau, did you have any correspondence with the outside world, any mail, anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I don't have it here. We were allowed to write one letter a week--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you write?

speaker: interviewee
text: --which was censored.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you-- what did you-- who did you write to, and what did you write?

speaker: interviewee
text: We couldn't write much, because it was strictly censored. And we were-- we were told what not to write.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost anything. All we could say-- we are fine. We could not complain. We could not-- if any-- we could not mention any sickness or any-- any other handicap, or whatever it is. We had to be fine, or we couldn't write. And if we wrote we weren't fine, they made sure we were not fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you receive any mail in to the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Once a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you get mail from?

speaker: interviewee
text: From my brother and my aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your brother at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lehrberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they write to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Similar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Theirs was censored, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Everything was censored. There was nothing which wasn't censored.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall any particular letter?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- as I said, they were meaningless, except we are here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you left the-- when you left Dachau, you went directly to Munich?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were put on a train and shipped to Munich and said we were on our own. I stayed there only overnight, until I went to Nuremberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Nuremberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six months later-- I-- four months-- five months later, I went to stay at a refugee camp in England.

speaker: interviewer
text: So during the five months you remained in Nuremberg? Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do during the those five months?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. I kept busy trying to get my things ready and to get passport and things like that. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: During that time, did you have any documents, different documents, inside of Germany that you had to use?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That came later.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had no papers or documents at this time, when you were released from Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you-- when were you given-- you said you came later. When did that come later?

speaker: interviewee
text: After I had left. That was the-- after the war started was they had special identification cards.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you left Germany when?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the 30th of July.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Or 30th of June, 1939. It's in my passport.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who did you leave with?

speaker: interviewee
text: By myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went by yourself. Your brother was not with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He had not-- he didn't have permission to come to the refugee camp, because not being in Dachau, he was not considered endangered.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you left by yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the refugee camp in England.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train and across the Channel.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was the trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: It took about almost a day. It wasn't my own. It was another transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went by yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, we were collected in Cologne and went there as a group, just for transportation purposes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these were all people going to the same refugee camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We were-- on that transport, we were about 30.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were these other people? Men your age?

speaker: interviewee
text: From all over Germany. I didn't know anyone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it men and women?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were men, but some were married and their wives were on the same train, but not with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had a passport?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the passport stamped? Or did it have anything unusual in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know it was unusual. It was, of course-- it had the J, and being Jewish and the visa to go to England. You had to have visas at that time. And my particular visa was restricted to that refugee camp. If I wanted to get out of it-- once visiting-- I went Edinburgh to visit a cousin who also was a refugee there. I had to get permission from the local police.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your visa was restricted to this refugee camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know whether it was official or unofficial. It was called the Kitchener Camp. I think officially it was a refugee-- Jewish refugee camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in the county of Kent near Richborough, not too far from Ramsgate.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe this refugee camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was an old British army barrack. That's where the name Kitchener Camp came from. It was used for the British Expeditionary Force in World War One. And it fell too-- and there was very little left of it, so it was bought by a Jewish relief organization-- mostly American money-- and restored, mostly with the help of the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were at the camp when you arrived, would you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. About a thousand, I would say. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't think it was able to hold more.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe the camp-- the refugee camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Primitive. More primitive than Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Toilet facilities, rooms-- rooms, of course. We had no heat in the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you sleep? What were the sleeping conditions?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were army cots.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was in charge?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a refugee-- run by a refugee company. I don't even know. I-- at that time, I didn't care much about anything anymore. I just drifted along wherever I was pushed. I was not much interested in my surroundings.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Yes, everybody of course had to help with the maintenance, chores, and so on. And being naturally inclined, I wound up taking-- in the hospital. We had a little hospital there-- being a helper.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you worked in the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of illnesses came in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly colds, maybe a few injuries. Serious ones were shipped to the local hospital in Ramsgate.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the people in charge were from a Jewish relief organization, did I understand?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There were two Englishmen, and the rest were, I think, [INAUDIBLE] from Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: German Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Refugees. Refugees. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you not allowed to leave? Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were allowed to leave within five miles of the camp. So I also had a bike I bought. I could go to Ramsgate. It was summer. I could go to the beach or go to Richborough, Sandwich, which was a close by town. That was permissible. And that five-mile limit was flexible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you recall anything else that occurred while you were in Kitchener?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were there-- I was there less than two months when the war in Europe broke out. And pretty soon afterwards, the British liked to-- would like to get the camp back. They [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] started out first recruiting some of us for the Pioneer Corps, which was a fancy name for a labor battalion. And they were actually able to form out of-- from the refugees to [INAUDIBLE], which in the beginning were part of the camp in one section. But later on, just after I left, they deported all non-military and interred some on the Isle of Man. So they went from one internment camp to another. And the British considered us Germans and treated us accordingly. They hated Jews to start out with, and they were not very fond of refugees. I could tell stories, mostly hearsay, some personal, about how the British react to the German refugees in England. It's not very salutary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell something--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that happened to you personally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, nothing happened to me, personally, but-- because I left. But quite a few others, they were interred in the Isle of Man and stayed there for two years, and so on. In that-- in the internment camp in that Isle of Man was not only people from the camp, but refugees, domestics mostly, who worked as domestics in England. That was the only type of job which was permitted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is the Isle of Man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Between England and Ireland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated personally by the British? Yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had very little contact, so I cannot complain much.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Half a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And during this time, you were not assigned? Or you were assigned to any tasks?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were-- it was a holding camp, waiting to get to [INAUDIBLE] to get out of it one way or another. It was just a temporary holding to get out of Germany until we were able to get someplace else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with anybody else you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: It happened [INAUDIBLE] and [? Steve ?] [INAUDIBLE] a friend of mine was also there. And he also came to the United States, and we've still stayed friends. And I knew a few local people. But otherwise they were just shadows.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't have much contact with the local population?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the circumstances that you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got my permit to enter the United States. My quota number came up, and I went to-- had to go to the consulate in London. I got my passport and visa, and I came to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- I left England on the 30th of January, 1939, and I arrived on the 12th of February.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: On a British ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was the Georgic.

speaker: interviewer
text: The--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Georgic-- G-E-O-R-G-I-G-- C. G-I-G. It was a Cunard liner.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you enter?

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the reasons I couldn't get there after I got out of Dachau, and I had enough money on me directly, from cash. So I could walk into a Cunard line office and building and bought a first class ticket on the Queen Mary to the United States, open-- an open ticket. With the help of that ticket, I got a ticket on the Cunard line. Most people at the Kitchener Camp had tickets-- open tickets to the United States. [DRINKS] But after the war broke out, the German lines, the French lines, the Italian lines, and all those which normally came from-- stopped in England and went to the United States, wouldn't stop there anymore. So all the tickets-- Cunard that was the only line which recognized its tickets.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had some money to come to the States. And many people were in camp with me. And when their quota number came up, they had no money to pay for the passage. I'm not sure I understand. You said you came on another ship.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [INAUDIBLE] But they-- yes, but they recognized their own line tickets, the tickets from their own line.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you couldn't come on the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't-- The Queen Mary, [INAUDIBLE]. So the Queen Mary is a troop ship now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came-- you were able to come on another ship.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I came on another ship on the Cunard line.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you come to in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: I landed in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see the Statue of Liberty?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for  you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was too numb to think anything. It was a period for me where I was just drifting along. I was not enthusiastic about anything. I just shuffled along.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: 21 At that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you came to New York--

speaker: interviewer
text: --who did you live with?

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle and my brother had come before. They were able to get directly out of Germany, get their quota number in October of '39. That was a month and a half after the war broke out in Europe, where many people were trapped in the other parts [INAUDIBLE]. So there were [INAUDIBLE] faster. And Holland still was a neutral country. And it was possible to take passage from Amsterdam on the Holland American line and even to pay with German money. So they came in October 1939. to the United States, directly, while I was still sitting in England waiting to be processed by the consulate.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you live in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in Newark. My-- my uncle and my brother lived in Newark.
